trajectory_summary: The call opens on a qualified, genuinely interested customer — existing system owner, 849 credit, clear add-on request — and ends without a locked appointment, a confirmed email address, or a specific callback time. Holland completes the orphaned-flow qualification efficiently but misses every structural commitment at the close. The call's energy never builds because Holland's opening posture minimizes the call's value rather than creating curiosity, and his exit posture accepts Ken's constraints without extracting any anchor.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the correct classification is 'tentative_callback_unanchored' — Ken agreed to continue the conversation in a couple of hours, but no callback time was locked, no email was confirmed on-call, and no appointment was set. This is structurally closer to a warm lead released than a booked outcome. No-show risk is elevated.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Okay. Yeah. No problem. We can pick it up in a couple hours then. You have a wonderful rest of your day.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland had a cooperative, qualified customer — 849 credit, clear add-on intent, agreed to continue — and released the call with zero structural anchors. No locked callback time, no confirmed email, no appointment. This is the single highest-leverage coaching gap on this call because it directly determines whether the sit happens at all. The fix is one sentence: 'I'll call you back at [specific time] — does 6 PM work?' Ken would have said yes. Holland didn't ask.

key_moments.quote: And then on Google Maps, it shows you have a pretty large tree in your backyard. Is that tree removed?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland asks about the tree as a shading/DQ check, which is correct procedure. But Ken's answer — 'That's why the panels are located on less than optimal location' — is a bill-pain adjacent signal Holland walks past entirely. Ken is telling Holland that his existing system is underperforming because of the tree. That's frustration with his current setup, which is the emotional anchor for the entire add-on pitch. Holland logs the answer and moves on without acknowledging the implication.

key_moments.quote: K. And then who are your panels with currently? What company did you buy them from?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland asks the required orphaned-flow question but never correctly confirms the answer. Ken spells 'Astrum' clearly (A-S-T-R-U-M) and Holland renders it first as 'A s t u r o n' (01:53) and then as 'T r u m' (01:59). Ken corrects both times. Holland acknowledges the second correction and moves on without repeating the name back to confirm. The orphaned_flow protocol requires capturing the existing company name to check against hard-DQ providers (Sunrun, Pink Solar). Holland never lands a clean confirmation.

key_moments.quote: And then I have down here that you're paying about 200 a month on that electric bill. Is that accurate?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland reads a pre-populated bill figure from the lead record rather than asking Ken to surface his own bill pain. Ken pushes back ('That sounds a little high') and Holland immediately concedes ('A little high, I would say') without any attempt to anchor the emotional weight of the number — even a lower number. The bill is captured as a data point and immediately dropped. No flip, no alignment-against-utility, no curiosity created.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. Just send me an email, and I'll send it to you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ken offers a cooperative path — he'll send the bill by email. Holland accepts but never confirms the email address on the call, never locks a callback time, and never sets any structural commitment. The result is a call that ends on a handshake with no verified channel and no anchor. When Holland calls back 'in a couple hours,' he has no confirmed email, no locked time, and no appointment — just Ken's goodwill, which may have cooled.
